items.question: What is the difference between axes-level and figure-level Seaborn functions?
items.answer: Axes-level functions (scatterplot, histplot, heatmap) draw on the current or supplied axes. Figure-level functions (relplot, catplot, displot, pairplot) create a full figure and may facet by col/row. Figure-level returns a FacetGrid or similar object; axes-level integrates with `fig, ax = plt.subplots()`.

items.question: How would you visualize a correlation matrix for numeric features?
items.answer: Compute Pearson correlation on numeric columns only: `corr = df.select_dtypes('number').corr()`. Plot with `sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm', center=0, square=True)`. Interpret symmetric cells as association, not causation.

items.question: When is pairplot appropriate, and when should you avoid it?
items.answer: Use pairplot for multivariate EDA on roughly 3–6 numeric features with an optional hue for class color. Avoid it on high-dimensional data— O(n²) panels become unreadable. Prefer feature selection or dimensionality reduction first for dozens of columns.

items.question: How does Seaborn relate to Matplotlib in a production notebook?
items.answer: Seaborn constructs Matplotlib figures. Call `sns.set_theme()` for styling, use Seaborn for statistical plots, then use Matplotlib (`plt.savefig`, `ax.set_xlabel`, tight_layout) for final tweaks. You can combine axes-level Seaborn on subplots created with `plt.subplots`.
